content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game. A single pawn is placed on a grid. The grid consists of cells **(X, Y)** for non-negative integers **X** and **Y**. Some cells of the grid have been marked as impassable. A move consists of choosing some positive integer **D** and moving the pawn from **(X, Y)** either to **(X − D, Y)** or to **(X, Y − D)**, of course, if this cell belongs to the grid. In other words, we move the pawn a positive number of steps along the row or column towards the origin. The pawn is not allowed to land on nor pass through an impassable cell. Alice and Bob alternate moves, with Alice going first. The first player unable to make a move loses.

Alice and Bob will play several games on the same grid, but with different starting positions of the pawn. Assuming both players play optimally, determine which player will win each game.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases will not exceed 100000
The sum of Q over all test cases will not exceed 100000
All coordinate values will be between 0 and 1000000000, inclusive
All impassable cells will be distinct
No starting position of the pawn will be impassable
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N, the number of impassable cells. N lines follow with 2 integers each: the X and Y coordinates of an impassable cell. Following this is a line with an integer Q, the number of games to be played on this grid. Q lines follow with 2 integers each: the X and Y coordinates of the pawn.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the winner of each of the Q games, one per line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first game starts at cell (2, 2). Alice can move to cell (1, 2), and then Bob has to move to cell (0, 2). Alice can then move to cell (0, 0), leaving Bob with no available moves. Therefore, Alice wins the first game. The second game starts at cell (1, 2). Bob can move to cell (0, 2), and then Alice has to move to cell (0, 1). Bob can then move to cell (0, 0), leaving Alice with no available moves. Therefore, Bob wins the second game. The third game starts at cell (3, 4). Alice can move to cell (2, 4), and then Bob has to move to cell (1, 4). Alice can then move to cell (0, 4), and then Bob has to move to cell (0, 3). Alice can then move to cell (0, 2), and then Bob has to move to cell (0, 1). Alice can then move to cell (0, 0), leaving Bob with no available moves. Therefore, Alice wins the third game. The fourth game starts at cell (6, 5). Bob can move to cell (5, 5), and then Alice has to move to cell (4, 5). Bob can then move to cell (3, 5), and then Alice has to move to cell (2, 5). Bob can then move to cell (1, 5), and then Alice has to move to cell (0, 5). Bob can then move to cell (0, 4), and then Alice has to move to cell (0, 3). Bob can then move to cell (0, 2), and then Alice has to move to cell (0, 1). Bob can then move to cell (0, 0), leaving Alice with no available moves. Therefore, Bob wins the fourth game.
The game starts at cell (1, 1). Since cell (0, 0) is impassable, Alice has no available moves. Therefore, Bob wins the game.

The game starts at cell (1, 1). Alice can move to cell (0, 1), and then Bob has to move to cell (0, 0). Alice can then move to cell (0, 0), leaving Bob with no available moves. Therefore, Alice wins the game.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming and game theory. We can create a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the winner of the game when the pawn is at position (i, j). We can fill up the dp array by iterating over all possible positions and checking if the current position is impassable or not. If it is impassable, we can't move to this position, so the winner will be the same as the winner of the previous position. If it is not impassable, we can move to this position and the winner will be the opposite of the winner of the previous position.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + Q)) per test case, O(T * (N + Q)) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + Q) — the dominant structure is the dp array.
